approaches.name: Approach 1: Recursive Brute Force O(2^n)
approaches.summary: This approach uses recursion to solve the Tower of Hanoi problem by breaking it down into subproblems: move n-1 disks from source to auxiliary, move the largest disk from source to destination, and then move n-1 disks from auxiliary to destination. It is the standard recursive solution that directly follows the problem's definition and prints all moves.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Recursive Tower of Hanoi Solution
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of Tower of Hanoi using recursion to print all moves for n disks.
ladders.explanation: The Tower of Hanoi problem involves moving n disks from source rod A to destination rod C using auxiliary rod B, following the rules: only one disk can be moved at a time, no larger disk can be placed on a smaller disk. The recursive solution elegantly breaks this problem into three subproblems: 1) Move n-1 disks from source to auxiliary (using destination as temporary), 2) Move the largest disk from source to destination, 3) Move n-1 disks from auxiliary to destination (using source as temporary). This creates a recursive tree where each call generates two more calls, leading to exponential growth. The intuition is that moving n disks requires moving n-1 disks twice plus one direct move, which naturally maps to recursion. The base case is when n=1, where we simply move the disk directly. Time complexity is O(2^n) because each call generates two recursive calls, creating a binary recursion tree with 2^n-1 moves. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack depth. This connects to L1 as the complete implementation of the recursive function. Common mistakes include incorrect parameter order in recursive calls, forgetting base case, or misunderstanding the role of auxiliary rod. Real code pattern: def hanoi(n, source, auxiliary, destination): if n == 1: print(f"Move disk 1 from {source} to {destination}"); return; hanoi(n-1, source, destination, auxiliary); print(f"Move disk {n} from {source} to {destination}"); hanoi(n-1, auxiliary, source, destination).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Move Function
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive function that moves disks between rods.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core recursive function hanoi(n, source, auxiliary, destination) that orchestrates all moves. The intuition is that moving n disks requires solving three smaller instances: moving n-1 disks to auxiliary, moving the largest disk to destination, and moving n-1 disks from auxiliary to destination. This recursive decomposition works because it maintains the constraint that no larger disk is placed on a smaller one—by always moving the largest disk directly when all smaller disks are out of the way. The algorithm: if n == 1, print move disk 1 from source to destination; else, recursively call hanoi(n-1, source, destination, auxiliary) to move n-1 disks to auxiliary, print move disk n from source to destination, then recursively call hanoi(n-1, auxiliary, source, destination) to move n-1 disks from auxiliary to destination. Time complexity for this function is O(2^n) as it makes 2^n-1 moves. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion depth. This connects to L0 as the complete implementation of this function solves the full problem. Common mistakes include swapping auxiliary and destination parameters in recursive calls, which breaks the logic. Real code pattern: def hanoi(n, src, aux, dst): if n == 1: print(f"Move disk 1 from {src} to {dst}"); else: hanoi(n-1, src, dst, aux); print(f"Move disk {n} from {src} to {dst}"); hanoi(n-1, aux, src, dst).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Recursive Decomposition Strategy
ladders.desc: Understand how to break Tower of Hanoi into three recursive subproblems.
ladders.explanation: The core logic is the recursive decomposition: to move n disks from source to destination using auxiliary, we solve three smaller problems: move n-1 disks from source to auxiliary, move the largest disk from source to destination, and move n-1 disks from auxiliary to destination. The intuition is that moving the largest disk requires all smaller disks to be on the auxiliary rod, creating a clear subproblem structure. This works because it reduces the problem size by one disk each time, eventually reaching the base case of moving a single disk. The algorithm in pseudocode: if n == 1, move disk directly; else, recursively solve for n-1 disks with swapped roles of auxiliary and destination, then move disk n, then recursively solve for n-1 disks with swapped roles of source and auxiliary. Time complexity analysis shows T(n) = 2T(n-1) + 1, which solves to O(2^n). Space complexity is O(n) for recursion stack. This connects to L1 as implementing this logic creates the recursive function. Common mistakes include not swapping rod roles correctly in recursive calls, leading to illegal moves. Real code pattern: The recursive calls hanoi(n-1, source, destination, auxiliary) and hanoi(n-1, auxiliary, source, destination) implement this decomposition.

examples.output: Decompose into: move 2 disks A->B using C, move disk 3 A->C, move 2 disks B->C using A
examples.trace: For n=3: First subproblem: move 2 disks from A to B using C (recursively decomposes further). Second: move disk 3 from A to C directly. Third: move 2 disks from B to C using A (recursively decomposes further).

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Function with Parameters
ladders.desc: Create a recursive function that takes disk count and rod names as parameters.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves writing a recursive function that accepts n (number of disks) and three rod names (source, auxiliary, destination) as parameters. The intuition is that recursion requires passing state—which disks to move and between which rods—through parameters. The function uses these parameters to determine what move to make and how to recurse. The algorithm: define function hanoi(n, src, aux, dst); if n == 1, print moving disk 1 from src to dst; else, call hanoi(n-1, src, dst, aux) [note swapped aux and dst], then print moving disk n from src to dst, then call hanoi(n-1, aux, src, dst) [note swapped src and aux]. Time complexity for this function call is O(2^n) in total moves, but each call is O(1) except recursive calls. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion depth. This connects to L2 as implementing this function with parameters enables the recursive decomposition. Common mistakes include incorrect parameter order in recursive calls, leading to logic errors. Real code pattern: def hanoi(n, src, aux, dst): if n == 1: print(f"Move disk 1 from {src} to {dst}"); else: hanoi(n-1, src, dst, aux); print(f"Move disk {n} from {src} to {dst}"); hanoi(n-1, aux, src, dst).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Print a Single Disk Move
ladders.desc: Output a formatted string for moving one disk from source to destination.
ladders.explanation: The basic operation is printing a move statement in the format 'Move disk X from ROD1 to ROD2', where X is the disk number and ROD1/ROD2 are rod names. This operation is performed each time a disk is moved, either in the base case (n=1) or when moving the largest disk in recursive steps. The intuition is that each move is a discrete action that must be recorded to solve the problem. The algorithm is straightforward: given disk number n and rod names source and destination, print the formatted string. Time complexity is O(1) per print. Space complexity is O(1) for the string. This connects to L3 as this operation is used within the recursive function to output moves. Common mistakes include incorrect formatting or mixing up disk numbers. Real code pattern: print(f"Move disk {disk_number} from {source_rod} to {destination_rod}").

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Recursion Base Case
ladders.desc: Learn to write a simple recursive function with a base case.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces recursion with a base case, which is essential for Tower of Hanoi. Recursion is a function calling itself with modified parameters until a base case stops it. The intuition is that complex problems can be solved by reducing them to simpler instances. For Tower of Hanoi, the base case is when n=1: move the disk directly. The algorithm: define a recursive function that checks if n == 1; if yes, perform the base action (e.g., print a move); if no, make recursive calls with n-1. Time complexity depends on the problem, but base case ensures termination. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion depth. This connects to L4 as the base case uses the basic print operation. Common mistakes include missing base case leading to infinite recursion, or incorrect base condition. Real code pattern: def recursive_func(n): if n == 1: print("Base case action"); else: recursive_func(n-1).

examples.output: Call recursive_func(3): n=3 not 1, call recursive_func(2): n=2 not 1, call recursive_func(1): n=1, print 'Base case action', return, return, return.

approaches.name: Approach 2: Iterative Simulation O(2^n)
approaches.summary: This approach simulates the recursive process iteratively using a stack or explicit rules to move disks between pegs without recursion. It follows the same logical steps as the recursive solution but avoids recursive function calls, which can be useful in environments with recursion depth limits.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Iterative Tower of Hanoi Solution
ladders.desc: Complete iterative implementation of Tower of Hanoi using explicit rules to move disks between pegs without recursion.
ladders.explanation: The iterative Tower of Hanoi solution simulates the recursive process using explicit rules based on the parity of the number of disks. The intuition is that we can achieve the same disk movements as the recursive solution by following deterministic rules: for odd-numbered disks, the smallest disk moves in a circular pattern (A→B→C→A), and for even-numbered disks, it moves in the opposite direction (A→C→B→A). Between moves of the smallest disk, we make the only other legal move available. This approach avoids recursion entirely, making it suitable for environments with recursion depth limits. The algorithm works by tracking the current state of three pegs and repeatedly applying these rules until all disks are moved from the source to the destination peg. Time complexity is O(2^n) since we make 2^n - 1 moves, and space complexity is O(n) for storing the peg states. This connects to the parent problem by providing a complete working solution. Common mistakes include incorrect parity handling and forgetting to check for legal moves. Real code pattern uses three stacks to represent pegs and loops until completion.

examples.output:
Move disk 1 from A to B
Move disk 2 from A to C
Move disk 1 from B to C
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize pegs: A=[2,1], B=[], C=[], total moves=3. Step 2: n=2 (even), smallest moves A→C→B→A. Step 3: Move smallest disk 1 from A to C (legal). Step 4: Make only legal move: disk 2 from A to B. Step 5: Move smallest disk 1 from C to B (legal). Step 6: Make only legal move: disk 2 from B to C. Step 7: Move smallest disk 1 from B to C (legal). Done.

Step 10: Legal move: disk 3 from B to C. Step 11: Move disk 1 from B to C. Done.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Determining Legal Moves Between Pegs
ladders.desc: Function to find the only legal move between two pegs that aren't holding the smallest disk.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is crucial because in the iterative Tower of Hanoi algorithm, after moving the smallest disk, we need to make the only other legal move available between the two pegs that don't contain the smallest disk. The intuition is that at any point, there are exactly three pegs, and one contains the smallest disk. Between the other two pegs, only one move is legal: moving the smaller disk from one peg to the other, provided the destination peg is empty or has a larger disk on top. The algorithm checks the top disks of the two candidate pegs and determines which move is valid. Time complexity is O(1) since it involves constant-time stack operations, and space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the parent problem by enabling the iterative simulation to progress correctly. Common mistakes include not handling empty pegs properly or incorrectly comparing disk sizes. Real code pattern uses peek operations on stacks to compare disk values.

examples.output: Move disk 1 from peg1 to peg2

ladders.title: Core Logic — Smallest Disk Movement Pattern Based on Parity
ladders.desc: Rule for moving the smallest disk in a circular pattern depending on whether total disks are odd or even.
ladders.explanation: The core insight of the iterative Tower of Hanoi is that the smallest disk always moves in a predictable circular pattern, and this pattern depends on the parity (odd/even) of the total number of disks. For an odd number of disks, the smallest disk moves from source to auxiliary to destination and back to source (A→B→C→A). For an even number of disks, it moves in the opposite direction: source to destination to auxiliary and back to source (A→C→B→A). This works because it mimics the recursive solution's behavior where the smallest disk alternates between pegs in a fixed cycle. The algorithm tracks the current peg of the smallest disk and uses modulo arithmetic to determine the next peg in the cycle. Time and space complexity are O(1) per move. This is the central technique that enables the iterative simulation. Common mistakes include reversing the patterns or not updating the current peg correctly. Real code pattern uses a dictionary or array to map the cycle.

examples.output: Move smallest disk to 'B'
examples.trace: Step 1: n=3 is odd, pattern is A→B→C→A. Step 2: Current peg is A. Step 3: Next in cycle: A→B. Step 4: Output move to B.

ladders.title: Building Block — Representing Pegs as Stacks
ladders.desc: Using stack data structures to model the pegs and disks in Tower of Hanoi.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves representing the three pegs in Tower of Hanoi as stacks, where each stack holds disks with the smallest disk (disk 1) on top. The intuition is that stacks naturally enforce the Tower of Hanoi rule: only the top disk can be moved, and a larger disk cannot be placed on a smaller one. By using stacks, we can easily push and pop disks to simulate moves. The algorithm initializes the source peg with disks in descending order (largest at bottom) and the other pegs as empty stacks. Time complexity for push/pop is O(1), and space complexity is O(n) for storing n disks. This feeds into the core logic by providing the data structure needed to track disk positions. Common mistakes include incorrect initialization order or not handling stack underflow. Real code pattern uses list or deque as stacks in Python.

examples.trace: Step 1: Create three empty stacks for A, B, C. Step 2: For i from n down to 1: push i onto stack A. Step 3: After pushes: A has 3 (bottom), then 2, then 1 (top). Step 4: B and C remain empty.
Step 1: Create stacks X, Y, Z. Step 2: Push 2 onto X. Step 3: Push 1 onto X. Step 4: Y and Z are empty.

One=[3,2,1], Two=[], Three=[]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Moving a Disk Between Two Stacks
ladders.desc: Function to pop a disk from one stack and push it onto another if the move is legal.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves moving the top disk from one peg (stack) to another, which is the basic action in Tower of Hanoi. The intuition is that we can only move a disk if the destination stack is empty or has a larger disk on top. The algorithm checks the top elements of both stacks: if the source is not empty, and either the destination is empty or the source's top disk is smaller than the destination's top disk, then pop from source and push to destination. Time complexity is O(1), and space complexity is O(1). This is used repeatedly in the iterative simulation. Common mistakes include not checking for empty stacks or incorrect comparison. Real code pattern uses peek and pop/push operations.

examples.trace: Step 1: Check src top: 1. Step 2: Check dest top: 2. Step 3: Compare: 1 < 2, so move is legal. Step 4: Pop 1 from src. Step 5: Push 1 onto dest. Step 6: New src=[3], dest=[2,1].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Stacks (LIFO Principle)
ladders.desc: Introduction to stack data structure and its Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) behavior.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: a stack is a linear data structure that follows the Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) principle, meaning the last element added is the first one removed. The intuition is like a stack of plates: you add plates to the top and remove from the top. In Tower of Hanoi, pegs are modeled as stacks because disks can only be moved from the top, mimicking LIFO behavior. Basic operations include push (add to top), pop (remove from top), and peek (view top without removing). Time complexity for these operations is O(1), and space complexity depends on the number of elements. This foundation is essential for representing pegs in the iterative solution. Common mistakes include confusing LIFO with FIFO or not handling empty stacks. Real code pattern in Python uses list with append() for push and pop() for pop.

examples.output: Stack after pushes: [3,2,1]; after first pop: [3,2]; after second pop: [3]
